coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electron–hole separation in SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure for efficient CO2 photoreduction

coredata.dc:description: The use of semiconductor photocatalytic reduction technology has higher efficiency and lower energy consumption for CO2 reduction, but its existence of some mismatched energy band positions and relatively slow charge-hole separation greatly limits its practical application. In this paper, a SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure model with carbon and nitrogen hybridization is proposed to gain an in-depth understanding of the role of hybridization and precious metal modification in photocatalysis. Carriers were investigated from the perspective of kinetics through photocurrent, LSV curve, and FL spectroscopy. After modification of Au nanoparticles, the photo-excited electrons showed a slower decay process, indicating that the separation of carriers was enhanced, which was further confirmed by impedance and PL spectroscopy. SEM, AFM and TEM analysis confirmed that the 2D face-to-face SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure was successfully prepared and the Au nanoparticles were evenly distributed. Au nanoparticles not only act as a bridge for electron transport in 2D semiconductor materials to accelerate electron transport, but generate more excited electrons by themselves under the radiation of sunlight, which effectively improves the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and enhances the photocatalyst activity. DFT calculations show that the Z-type mechanism and the intercalated energy level of the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure expand the light absorption and improve the carrier separation efficiency. As expected, the photocatalyst activity with CO and CH4 the evolution rate of up 93.81 µmol·g−1·h−1 and 74.98 µmol·g−1·h−1 under visible light irradiation, respectively, while the photocatalytic activity is negligible loss after 30 h photoreduction. This work reveals the role of embedded structure and precious metal modification in the separation and transmission of electron-hole, opening up new perspective for achieving high-efficiency solar CO2 reduction performance.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electron–hole separation in SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure for efficient CO2 photoreduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy band location and charge separation mechanism of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 system during photoreduction under sunlight irradiation.

The use of semiconductor photocatalytic reduction technology has higher efficiency and lower energy consumption for CO2 reduction, but its existence of some mismatched energy band positions and relatively slow charge-hole separation greatly limits its practical application. In this paper, a SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure model with carbon and nitrogen hybridization is proposed to gain an in-depth understanding of the role of hybridization and precious metal modification in photocatalysis. Carriers were investigated from the perspective of kinetics through photocurrent, LSV curve, and FL spectroscopy. After modification of Au nanoparticles, the photo-excited electrons showed a slower decay process, indicating that the separation of carriers was enhanced, which was further confirmed by impedance and PL spectroscopy. SEM, AFM and TEM analysis confirmed that the 2D face-to-face SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure was successfully prepared and the Au nanoparticles were evenly distributed. Au nanoparticles not only act as a bridge for electron transport in 2D semiconductor materials to accelerate electron transport, but generate more excited electrons by themselves under the radiation of sunlight, which effectively improves the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and enhances the photocatalyst activity. DFT calculations show that the Z-type mechanism and the intercalated energy level of the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure expand the light absorption and improve the carrier separation efficiency. As expected, the photocatalyst activity with CO and CH4 the evolution rate of up 93.81 µmol·g−1·h−1 and 74.98 µmol·g−1·h−1 under visible light irradiation, respectively, while the photocatalytic activity is negligible loss after 30 h photoreduction. This work reveals the role of embedded structure and precious metal modification in the separation and transmission of electron-hole, opening up new perspective for achieving high-efficiency solar CO2 reduction performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial photosynthesis is the most abundant and renewable energy sources that are expected to become an eco-friendly alternative to fossil fuels in the future [1]. Photocatalysts are directly able to obtain energy from sunlight in this process while converting carbon dioxide (CO2) into hydrocarbons, solving the energy crisis and environmental issues [2,3]. Photoreduction of CO2 to additional carbon fuel based on solar energy processes requires very efficient and stable catalytic materials [4]. Heretofore, various semiconductors have been studied for photoreduction of CO2, such as TiO2, ZnO, ZnIn2S4, MoS2 and In2S3 [5–10]. However, the large band gap and high electron-hole recombination rate of most these semiconductor materials lead to the low photocatalytic efficiency. In order to defect these limitations and improve the efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction, semiconductors have been modified and improved through a variety of strategies: regulation of nanostructures, hybridization with carbonaceous materials, modification of precious metals and doping [11–13]. Although these heterogeneous hybrid semiconductors have enhanced the catalyst activity, the quantum efficiency and catalytic selectivity are far from reaching industrial standards [14]. In addition, the most commonly used narrow band gap semiconductor CdS brings another trouble thank to toxicity [15]. In terms of photocatalytic reduction of CO2, we hope to synthesize an eco-friendly hybrid nanostructured material and move forward on the basis of CdS [16,17]. In addition to using the high absorption coefficient and critical narrow band gap to maximum the use of solar energy, effective electronic separation is another significant factor for maintaining high activity in the photocatalytic system [18]. As such, controlling the diffusion path of carriers and constructing unique semiconductor nanostructures are critical factors for designing efficient photocatalytic systems [19].

Since graphene was discovered, two-dimensional (2D) layered transition metal disulfide nanostructures are playing a significant role in other field due to their electronic properties and extensive optical [20–22]. Besides, the low charge recombination and high surface area of 2D semiconductors can potentially increase photocatalyst activity. In various transition metal sulfides, it is called mosaic gold tin disulfide (SnS2), which is a natively existing narrow band gap (2.1–2.3 eV) semiconductor [23,24]. In recent years, it has been proven to have potential application value, and it can be used as an absorption layer for dye-sensitized solar cells, gas sensors, photovoltaic elements, and energy conversion and storage [25–27]. The appropriate band gap and good chemical stability of the atomic layer structure SnS2 provide two advantages for an excellent photocatalysts under visible light. For example, Jiao et al. [23] reported that the partially oxidized SnS2 atomic layer achieved efficient CO2 reduction; Qu et al. [28] designed porous coral-like SnS2, which showed good activity in removing Cr (VI) ions from water and photocatalytic reduction; Shown et al. used the micro-strain of the crystalline structure caused by carbon-doped SnS2 to achieve high-efficiency photocatalytic activity. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic output is far below the actual requirements due to the rapid recombination of carriers. To solve this problem, it is extremely necessary to control the diffusion path of carriers and reduce recombination of carriers by constructing hybrid embedded nanostructures between semiconductors [29]. Carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted much attention in photocatalysis thanks to its broad light absorption and excellent stability [25,30,31]. The face-to-face van der Waal heterojunction nanostructure composed of SnS2 and g-C3N4 has good energy band matching and interface effect, and the Z-type mechanism formed between semiconductors has good photoreduction ability [32]; when the carriers are transferred, an internal electric field is formed in a balanced state, and the interface effect shortens the diffusion distance of the carriers, and provides a good channel for the rapid transmission of electrons, thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency [33]. Recently, numerous articles have been reported on the coupling of precious metal nanoparticles and semiconductors to promote carriers’ the separation efficiency. For example, Li et al. [34] prepared sulfur-doped g-C3N4/Au/CdS Z-type heterojunction to enhance the photocatalyst activity; Bera et al. [12] used the size-dependent plasma effect to study the catalyst activity in Pt/TiO2 photocatalytic CO2 conversion reaction; He et al. synthesized the ZnO/Au hybrid nanostructure having remarkable photocatalytic and antibacterial activity. Moreover, these noble metal modification sites act as acceptor centers for electrons and holes, and inhibit carriers’ recombination during the transport of photo-generated carrier by reason of the coupling between the noble metal particles and the semiconductor [15]. Interestingly, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of precious metal plays a vital role in the optoelectronics and sensing of semiconductors [35,36].

Based on the above analysis, we first constructed a novel 2D/Au/2D SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure by solvothermal and deposition methods for served to photoreduction CO2 under the UV–vis light. It is worth noting that the synthesis process does not involve any surfactants or toxic substances, and Au nanoparticles not only act as receptors for photo-generated carrier inside the material, but also act as a bridge for rapid carrier transfer at the interface, speeding up electron transmission, thus reducing carrier recombination rate and improving the conversion efficiency of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 [34]. Furthermore, SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure is formed through the coupling of weak van der Waals forces, with the increase of g-C3N4, abundant surface functional groups are introduced and electrostatic interaction at the 2D/2D interface reduces the diffusion path and increases the surface area, thereby further improving the adsorption capacity, while the embedded structure optimizes the light absorption range and band gap structure of SnS2 [37]. Meanwhile, the internal electric field of the interface constructed by the unique embedded structure reduces the obstacles of electron transmission and promotes the transmission of carriers on the interface [38]. Notably, the Z-type carriers transport mechanism caused by the combination of the electrons in SnS2 and the holes in g-C3N4 improves the utilization and extraction of photo-generated electrons in g-C3N4 under photoexcitation conditions, thereby enhancing the photocatalyst activity [39,40]. Scheme 1 shows the entire process of manufacturing SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 and the total density of state (DOS) of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterostructure exhibits energy level interleaving, which can promote the separation of photo-generated carriers, thus effectively improve the photocatalytic activity.

Tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O), sodium dodecyl benzenesulfonate (SDBS), L-cysteine (C3H7NO2), urea, ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), ethanol(C2H5OH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), nitric acid (HNO3) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous sodium molybdate was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All reagents used for preparation and direct use were of analytical grade and were not further purified. Deionized water was used in the whole experiments from local sources.

The typical preparation process of g-C3N4, as follows: 10 g urea as raw material was put into the aluminum oxide crucible sealed with tin foil and calcined at 550 °C for 4 h in air (5 °C/min). Then, as-prepared sample was magnetic stirring in the HNO3 aqueous solution (pH = 1) at 80 ℃ for 8 h. Next, the obtained sample got purified with deionized water and ethanol for several times until the pH value was neutral. After that, the as-prepared samples were calcined in air for the second time at 500 °C for 2 h (5 °C/min). Finally, it is obvious that the color of the sample has varied from yellow to light yellow or even white, which means that the few-layer 2D g-C3N4 were obtained.

A slight change was made in a typical modified water bath process, SnCl4·5H2O (0.25 mmol), SDBS (0.6 mmol) and L-cysteine (2 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solution of 20 ml of ethylene glycol and 10 ml of deionized water, and stir to form a uniform solution for 10 min. Then, it was transferred to a 50 ml autoclave maintaining at 160 °C for 12 h. Preparing an atomic layer of SnS2 and collecting light yellow SnS2 samples, washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and put in the vacuum freeze-drying equipment until lyophilized for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SnS2/Au/ g-C3N4 embedded structure
ce:section.ce:para: SnS2/g-C3N4 was synthesized as follows: SnCl4·5H2O (0.25 mmol), SDBS (0.6 mmol) and L-cysteine (2 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solution of 20 ml of ethylene glycol and 10 ml of deionized water, and add a certain amount of g-C3N4, and stir to form a uniform solution for 10 min. Then, it was transferred to a 50 ml autoclave maintaining at 160 °C for 12 h. Preparing SnS2/g-C3N4 samples, washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and put in the vacuum freeze-drying equipment until lyophilized for further characterization.

SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 was synthesized as follows: Appropriate amount of SnS2/g-C3N4 was added to distilled water of 50 ml in beaker, and ultrasound to form a uniform solution for 30 min. Then, a certain amount (1, 3, 5 and 7 ml) of HAuCl4 aqueous solution (0.025 mol/L) was dropped into the SnS2/g-C3N4 turbid liquid. The suspension was stirred under the irradiation of an UV-light for 30 min. After completion of the reaction. Subsequently, the precipitate was washed by deionized water and ethanol for several times, and transferred to oven to dry at 80 ℃.
2.5 The specific characterization methods, photo-electrochemical test, calculation of Density Functional Theory (DFT) and photo-reduction CO2 performance test are shown in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure and surface chemical state
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows the XRD of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 with different g-C3N4 molar ratios and different contents of Au. The significant peaks at ca. 13.23° and 27.35° correspond to tri-s-triazine repeated units of the in-plane (100) and conjugated aromatic systems’ interlayer stacking (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4 phase (JCPDS 87-1526), respectively [30]. The obvious peaks at ca. 28.25° and 50.02° correspond to the SnS2 phase’s (100) and (110) crystal planes, respectively, and the lattice constants (a = 3.05 Å and c = 1.82 Å) [41]. The obvious peaks at ca. 38.16° and 44.39° correspond to the crystal planes (111) and (200) of Au species (JCPDS 80-0019) [34]. The results show that all diffraction peaks of SnS2 and g-C3N4 correspond to the standard XRD, which proves that the material is successfully synthesized. In addition, the different molar ratios of SnS2/g-C3N4 composites were studied. The diffraction peak positions of the two materials appeared in XRD and the position and intensity of the diffraction peaks altered slightly, indicating that the heterojunction is formed between the semiconductors by weak Van der Waals forces and similar results have been reported in other literatures [42]. Next, when Au nanoparticles are introduced, the position and intensity of other peaks remained unchanged, showing that the addition of Au nanoparticles did not break the internal structure of the material, and the Au diffraction peaks appeared, which indicates the success of the ternary embedded structure preparation [34]. Fig. 1b shows the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 XRD patterns of different Au contents. The intensity of the diffraction peaks corresponding to Au nanoparticles increases with their content, indicating that Au nanoparticles are embedded in the semiconductor.

To further determine the presence of g-C3N4, SnS2 and Au, the bonding state and surface electronic state of the synthetic materials are characterized by XPS. The full spectrum of Fig. 2 a confirmed the existence of Sn, S, C, N and Au elements, and the peak positions of Sn 3d, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and Au 4f coincide with the standard peaks. In Fig. 2b, the Sn 3d spectra of pure SnS2 has two diverse peaks, with the central peaks at 486.67 eV and 495.10 eV due to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, proving that Sn element exists in the chemical form of Sn4+ [43]. It is worth noting that after combining with g-C3N4, the Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 slightly downshift to 486.12 eV and 494.55 eV thank to the increase in the electronic density of Sn4+ [44]. At the same time, the introduction of Au nanoparticles in SnS2/g-C3N4 caused a slight downward shift in the bond energy position, leading to an increase in the electron density of the material [45]. In Fig. 2c, the central binding energies at 161.72 eV and 162.90 eV are attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [46]. With the introduction of g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles, a slight change in the bond energy position corresponds to a change in electron density. Meanwhile, the small peak at the position of 164.21 eV indicates the existence of S-N bonds, which further confirms the formation of SnS2/g-C3N4 embedded heterojunction [32]. The C 1s electron energy spectra was indicated in Fig. 2d, the g-C3N4, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 are fitted to two peaks at approximately 284.40–284.57 and 287.87–288.27 eV, which may originate from CC and NCN, respectively [47]. The N 1s of the core level spectra in Fig. 2e, the four peaks of pure g-C3N4 fitted by N 1s spectra are at 398.32, 399.77, 400.78 and 404.15 eV, which are attributed to NH, N(C)3, CNC and π-excitations, respectively [37]. The triazine ring’ the sp2-hybrid nitrogen exists in the form of C-NC, the tertiary nitrogen is coupled to three carbon in the form of N(C)3, insufficient thermal polycondensation and CN conjugated structures‘ π excitation lead to the formation of amino CNH bonds [48]. Fig. 2f displays the XPS spectra of Au 4f, the energy spectra at 83.35 eV and 87.02 eV correspond to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, respectively; indicating the existence of Au0 in the sample [49]. The above analysis further suggests that the embedded heterojunction has been triumphantly synthesized, and Au may serve as carrier transmission bridge between two semiconductors, thereby improving the efficiency of carrier separation. The embedded heterojunction proved by XPS corresponds to the result of XRD analysis.

Raman spectroscopy is commonly used to study the vibration characteristics and phase composition of materials. According to the Raman spectra of pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 3 ), the obvious peaks are situated in 475 cm−1, 705 cm−1, 975 cm−1 and 1234 cm−1, corresponding to the vibration mode of the CN heterocyclic ring in g-C3N4 [39]. In addition, in the SnS2 and SnS2/g-C3N4 composites, the apparent peak at 314 cm−1 coincides with the out-of-plane tensile A1g vibration mode of SnS2, which means that SnS2 successfully grows on the surface of g-C3N4 [50]. Interestingly, the peak of the Sn-S bond in the Raman spectra of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 is significantly reduced, which further indicates that the Au species may embed in the middle of two types of semiconductors and acts as a bridge for carrier transfer [51].

To ascertain the surface composition and functional groups of the sample, FT-IR used to explore and analyze the samples. The wide absorption band at 3100–3400 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H and O–H vibration modes (Fig. 4 a), which come from uncondensed amine groups on the surface and adsorbed water molecules [44]. The spike at 811 cm−1 is the triazine ring’s characteristic vibration bending out of the plane. The absorption bands at 1635 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1 can be assigned to the CN stretching. In Fig. 4b, the absorption bands at 1463, 1411, 1326, and 1254 cm−1 may be ascribed to C-N. Interestingly, the typical graphitized structure of carbon nitride is well preserved after g-C3N4 is introduced homogeneously. However, the peak at about 3100–3400 cm−1 is wider and stronger than g-C3N4. In addition, the absorption band at 811 cm−1 became sharper, indicating that more uncondensed amino and hydroxyl groups are fell off or replaced [52]. The position of SnS2/g-C3N4 material is slightly red-shifted relative to g-C3N4. The g-C3N4 conjugate structure is stretched to form a highly conjugated system, the reason may be that the introduced g-C3N4 establishes a surface chemical bond through the formation of S-N, which causes the significant charge transmission between SnS2 and g-C3N4 [53]. Moreover, the addition of g-C3N4 did not alter the chemical structure of SnS2, further explaining the formation of embedded structure.

The morphology and crystal structure of the prepared materials are confirmed by drawing photograph with SEM, TEM, AFM, HRTEM and EDX. Fig. 5 a and 5b show the obtained TEM images of g-C3N4 and SnS2, respectively. The results show that g-C3N4 and SnS2 self-assemble to form 2D sheet structure. Meanwhile, the average thickness of g-C3N4 is about 9 nm, and the average thickness of SnS2 is about 10 nm, which provides a good platform for the successful loading of nanoparticles (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 5c, SnS2 forms a face-to-face embedded structure under the control of different molar ratios of g-C3N4 and finds the optimal molar ratio for maximum activity. Fig. 5d displays the TEM of the prepared SnS2/Au/g-C3N4. The Au nanoparticles in the composite material have good dispersibility, which it retains a good surface-surface embedded structure after modification with Au nanoparticles [54]. The SEM image shown in Fig. S2 clearly shows regular g-C3N4 and SnS2 nanoflakes. SEM image and Mapping analysis of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 5e-j. Mapping images show the uniform distribution of Sn, S, C, N and Au elements (Fig. 5 f, g, h, i, j), confirming the successful preparation of nanocomposites, and most of the introduced Au nanoparticles exist between two nanoscale semiconductors. In the EDS image (Fig. 51), the Sn, S, C, N and Au elements are further confirmed in the prepared hybrid material. The HRTEM is displayed in Fig. 5k, the heterojunction and crystal form of the synthesized SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 can be obviously observed, and it matches commendably with SnS2 and Au nanoparticles; The lattice spacing is 0.31 nm and 0.24 nm correspond to the typical SnS2 crystal plane (100) and the classical Au nanoparticles crystal plane (111) [34]. In addition, the SAED characterization of the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 (Fig. 5m), the polycrystalline structure of the material is characterized by concentric rings and scattering diffraction patterns, which corresponds to the XRD results [43], further confirming the successful preparation of embedded structure.

To ascertain the position of the semiconductor band edge relative to the redox potential of the reaction, one further studied its optical absorption characteristics by UV–visible diffuse reflection method. Fig. 6 a shows the UV–vis DRS of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 samples and the Kubelka-Munk equation based on direct band gap material of [F(R∞)hν]1/2-hν conversion to calculate forbidden band width (Eg) of semiconductors [55]. Pure g-C3N4 and pure SnS2 samples show strong light absorption at about 450 nm and 530 nm, which correspond to the band gaps of approximately 2.71 eV and 2.12 eV, respectively (in the inset of Fig. 6a), corresponding to the previous reports [56]. Different from the absorption state of sole g-C3N4 and pure SnS2, the obtained SnS2/g-C3N4 composite shows an extensive scope of light absorption and a powerful absorption peak, which is resulted by the hybridization of g-C3N4 and SnS2. Obviously, after the introduction of Au nanoparticles, SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 composites displayed wider absorption of light than SnS2 and SnS2/g-C3N4, which makes clear that Au nanoparticles can significantly broaden range of light absorption and may further enhance the separation of carriers. The effective masses of the conduction band's bottom and the valence band's top are derived from the band structure. Especially, the opposing energy levels of the CBM (conduction band minimum) and VBM (valence band maximum) have an important effect on the redox ability of light-induced carriers in photo-reduction systems [57]. To further make sure the position of the band edge of the reaction redox potential, one measured its VB position using valence band spectra (VBs). The VB maximum of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are 1.48 eV and 1.52 eV, respectively (Fig. 6b). According to the band gap in the inset of Fig. 6a and the position of the XPS VB measured in Fig. 6b, the near band edge locations of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are plotted relative to normal hydrogen electrode. As can be seen from Fig. S3, both samples have the ability to simultaneously reduce CO2 and release O2. Besides, the location of CB of g-C3N4 and SnS2 is further characterized by Mote Nitro curves. As shown in Fig. S4 (a, b), the positions of conduction bands of pure g-C3N4 and pure SnS2 are −0.15 eV and −0.55 eV, which are fundamentally the same as empirical formula [58]. Besides, the total DOS and energy band structure of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterostructure are calculated (Fig. 6c). The interaction between SnS2 and g-C3N4 results in the energy band levels being interlaced, which is the experimental test result of the energy band structure is consistent. The interlaced energy level of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterostructure can promote the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus effectively improving the photocatalytic activity.

To further study the role of g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles in SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 photocatalyst. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) test is performed on the samples, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of pure g-C3N4, pure SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4, and their pore size distributions of Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) are shown in Fig. S5. In the light of the IUPAC classification, the relative pressure (p/p0) is in the range of 0.2–0.8 [59], the sample appeared a typical H3 hysteresis loop and a typical type IV isotherm, indicating the presence of mesopores [60]. Table S1 shows the relationship between specific surface area (SSA) and morphology. For pure g-C3N4, the SSA and total pore volume are 60.865 m2g−1 and 0.232 cm3g−1; for pure SnS2, it is 69.941 m2g−1 and 0.382 cm3g−1; for SnS2/g-C3N4, it is 73.276 m2g−1 and 0.391 cm3g−1; for SnS2/Au/g-C3N4, it is 109.061 m2g−1 and 0.663 cm3g−1. It can be distinctly shown that the ultra-thin two-dimensional structure of g-C3N4 and the size of Au nanoparticles improve the dispersion of SnS2 materials, resulting in most of SnS2 being effectively activated and utilized, which is beneficial for CO2 adsorption [32]. All the results show that g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles combine well with SnS2 to form embedded structure, and the increased SSA is conducive to generating more active sites and adsorbing more saturated gas on its surface, enhancing photocatalytic activity.

To research the effect of the introduced g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles on the carrier separation and recombination behavior, the photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy study is conducted. Fig. 7 a displays the PL spectrum of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 samples. Pure SnS2 material shows weak blue emission at 463 nm under 375 nm excitation, and pure g-C3N4 material shows strong purple emission at 433 nm under 375 nm excitation, which is chiefly due to the tall free recombination rate of g-C3N4 [52,61]. Moreover, the signal peak of pure g-C3N4 is higher than that of SnS2/g-C3N4 composite materials, showing that the recombination of SnS2/g-C3N4 is lower than that of pure g-C3N4. After the addition of Au nanoparticles, the luminous intensity of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 is obviously smaller than that of SnS2/g-C3N4, because it extends the life of carrier and reduces the recombination of carrier, implying that SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 composites have high activity, which is ascribed to the electron transmission achieved by the spatial expansion of Au nanoparticles and the embedded conjugate system [62]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence lifetimes of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4, and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 are tested by transient fluorescence spectrum. Compared with the other two photocatalysts, SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 has a faster decay kinetics. The radiative lifetime has been counted by fitting the double exponential equation (Fig. 7b) [63]. It serves to show that the lifetimes of the specimens are 2.80 ns, 3.30 ns, and 4.58 ns, respectively, of which the lifetime of emission (2.80 ns) of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 is shorter than that of g-C3N4 (3.30 ns) and SnS2/g-C3N4 (4.58 ns), it shows that the number of electrons generated by excitation increases sharply and the migration is obviously accelerated. Synchronously, the obvious PL quenching and lifetime reduction indicate the transfer path of electrons formed by SnS2 CB and holes formed by g-C3N4 VB to Au nanoparticles [34]. The relatively high decay life of the introduced g-C3N4 indicates that the interface charge transfer is faster in photocatalytic systems. Meanwhile, for the introduction of Au nanoparticles, the effective interfacial electron transfer of the hybrid material was significantly accelerated thanks to the ultrafast electron transmission process and reduced recombination of carrier in SnS2/g-C3N4 interface layer, this may be due to the construction of the embedded structure to shorten the diffusion time and reduce the average lifetime of carrier, so that they reach the site of the reaction prior to losing energy [15]. In conclusion, the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst has the best photocatalytic activity under light irradiation.

To better research the electron transport mechanism of the obtained materials, the photoelectrochemical performance of the photocatalyst is analyzed on the electrochemical workstation of the solar simulator (xenon lamp, 300 W). The response of photoelectric and LSV curve are shown in Fig. 8 , the transient photocurrent responses of pure g-C3N4, pure SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 are measured in 1 M Na2SO4 solution under simulated sunlight. SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 show a stronger photocurrent response of transient, which is about twice that of SnS2/g-C3N4 (Fig. 8a). This result suggests that Au nanoparticles play a vital role in efficient charge transfer, and ultimately photoexcitation electrons of Au nanoparticles also serve as one of the causes for enhancing the photoelectric response [64]. Noticeably, the photocurrent loss (DI) is very small during light irradiation, which may be reasonably ascribed to electron consumption, including the recombination of carrier and electron capture by O2 [65]. In addition, the electrochemical characteristics of pure g-C3N4, pure SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 electrodes are studied by measuring current and voltage (J-V, Fig. 8b). Generally speaking, high photocurrent signal means more effective transfer and separation of carriers, because photocurrent is mainly triggered by the diffusion of light-induced electrons. For the original g-C3N4 and SnS2, the photocurrent signal increases slower than SnS2/Au/g-C3N4, and as the applied voltage increases, the difference becomes larger. Compared with the Ag/AgCl reference electrode, the photocurrent intensity increased and reached 0.80 mA cm−2 at 1.0 V, which was 4.3 times and 4.0 times higher than g-C3N4 and SnS2, respectively, indicating that more electrons participated in the reduction of adsorbed CO2 molecules [66]. At the same time, the EIS test is analyzed (Fig. S6), the arc radius of the SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 is smaller than the original g-C3N4, SnS2 and SnS2/g-C3N4 electrode arc radius, indicating that SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 has a rapid response on the working electrode surface. Therefore, the above results further indicate that the introduction of g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles in the process of photoreduction of CO2 can well promote photogenerated charge transfer, thereby enhancing the activity of photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-reduction activity and cycle ability test
ce:section.ce:para: The photoreduction activity of the material is tested using gas chromatography under simulated sunlight using CO2 as a model reactant. Before the test, the adsorption and desorption equilibrium of all materials was finished by magnetic stirring under closed dark conditions and without air for 20 min. The catalytic activity of the synthesized embedded SnS2/g-C3N4 is not optimal (Fig. 9 ). By introducing different amounts of Au nanoparticles as a carrier for electron transport, the photocatalytic activity is significantly improved. Under simulated sunlight, the successfully synthesized SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 material shows the best activity, and the yields of CO and CH4 reached 469.07 µmol/g and 374.89 µmol/g at 5 h, respectively. When the excess dose of Au is about 5 ml and 7 ml, the photocatalytic performance is reduced, which may be due to the excessive Au nanoparticles glued to the surface of SnS2 and g-C3N4 limiting the light absorption of the catalyst and reducing the contact area under simulated CO2 photoreduction process [67]. Meanwhile, the contact angle of these photocatalysts was tested. As shown in Fig. S7, the hydrophilicity is in the sequence of g-C3N4 < SnS2 < SnS2/g-C3N4 < SnS2/Au/g-C3N4, the results show that the successfully synthesized ternary material is very soluble in water, it provided a good basis for photoreduction of CO2 in water solvent [68]; the CO2 adsorption behavior is in the sequence of g-C3N4 < SnS2 < SnS2/g-C3N4 < SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 (Fig. S8), the results show that the ternary composite material is favorable for CO2 adsorption and exhibits better photocatalytic activity. In addition, the internal electric field formed in the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure provides a fast transmission channel for Au nanoparticles, and the synergistic effect of the interface improves the photoelectric performance, and the same conclusion can be obtained from high resolution (Fig. 5k). The zeta potential (Table S2) characterization determines the polarity of the semiconductor surface charge and the increase in the absolute value of the potential indicates that the system tends to be more stable. As such, the enhancement of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 photocatalytic activity is thanks to the existence and uniform distribution of Au nanoparticles, and with the emergence of more carriers and excited electrons, SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 showed the best activity for CO2 photoreduction under the sunlight irradiation [69]. Meanwhile, it is found that there is a trace of hydrogen in the reaction process through the TCD detection, but it may be directly burned in the FID detection, resulting in the absence of hydrogen peaks in the gas chromatography (Fig. S9). Analysis of liquid-phase products by LC-MS revealed that no carbon-containing products are detected in the reaction products except for the sacrificial agent TEOA. This may be because there were no other carbon-containing products or the content was too low to detect.

As we all know, reusability and stability are significant criteria for judging the photocatalysts’ quality and further energy application. The composite material also has good cycle performance, which has been proved to perform six consecutive photocatalytic reduction CO2 cycle experiments on SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 samples. The experimental results (Fig. S10), we can expressly see that the photocatalytic efficiency of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 decreased slightly during the six photoactivity tests for CO2 reduction under the UV–vis irradiation, and almost no deactivation [70]. To further research the photocatalyst stability, Fig. S10 shows the XRD of SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 before and after six consecutive CO2 solution reduction experiments. The main characteristic diffraction peak of SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 is almost in the same position as the standard diffraction peak pattern, indicating that the structure composition has not altered after the cycle test. Furthermore, the intensity of the photocatalyst is slightly reduced compared to the sample before repeated photoreduction. Therefore, the prepared SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 can be identified as a stable photocatalyst, and Au nanoparticles play a vital role in exciting electrons and enhancing photo-generated carrier for effective separation [71].

In order to further determine the active substance of the photocatalytic process, electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to verify the existence of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) in the photocatalyst under UV–vis light illumination [35]. Fig. 10 shows that before and during simulation sunlight exposure, the ESR spectrum contains various capture agents from g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 hybrid materials. As a comparison, different materials were subjected to the same ESR test. Obviously, before the lamp is lit, there is no diffraction peak in Fig. 10 (a, b). However, when the lamp is on, the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4 and SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 spectra in Fig. 10 (a, b) quickly appear, such as the classical 1: 2: 2: 1 quartet feature peak and the observed obvious characteristics correspond to (DMPO- •O2 −) and (DMPO- •OH), respectively; and the signal peaks of •OH and •O2 − have significantly changed. The results display that oxygen can react with photo-generated electrons to form •O2 − [72]. Then, these materials detected that •O2 − further reacted with electrons and H+ to obtain •OH radicals, where H+ originated from the water’s ionization [73]. The introduction of g-C3N4 and Au nanoparticles respectively significantly enhanced the peak intensity of these two free radicals, which indirectly proves that the catalyst activity of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 is better than g-C3N4, SnS2 and SnS2/g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism in the photoreduction system
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above results, compared with the photoactivity of g-C3N4, SnS2, SnS2/g-C3N4, SnS2/3ml-Au/g-C3N4 hybrid nanomaterials show the best photocatalytic activity and have obvious enhancement signals in the generation of superoxides, hydroxyl groups, and light-induced cavities, which are related to the face-to-face embedded heterojunction formed by g-C3N4 and the uniformly distributed Au nanoparticles. Noteworthily, the face-to-face 2D embedded structure formed by SnS2 and g-C3N4 not only forms an internal electric field at the interface, but also provides a good channel for the rapid transmission of electrons [74]. In addition, Au nanoparticles act as a bridge for electron transmission and generate more excited electrons, thereby speeding up electron transport and increasing the catalytic activity of the composite [75]. According to the energy band engineering, the band positions of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are computed by empirical formulas (ECB = EVB − Eg and EVB = X − Ee + 0.5Eg ) [76]. The CB and VB of pure SnS2 with forbidden band width (Eg = 2.12 eV) are computed to be −0.52 and 1.60 eV (relative to NHE, Fig. 11 a), respectively. The conduction and valence band edges of pure g-C3N4 with a wide band gap width (Eg = 2.71 eV) are calculated to be −1.50 and 1.21 eV (relative to NHE), respectively. It is crisply seen through DRS that the band gap of SnS2 becomes markedly narrowed behind the introduction of g-C3N4 (Fig. 6a). Therefore, the absorption range of the wavelength is extended, enhancing the visible light’s absorption. Noticeably, after the formation of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterojunction, the CB location of the hybrid SnS2 is lower, which the VB location is smaller than the positive value of original SnS2. An analogous trend can be saw in the catalytic activity in Fig. 9. Moreover, the CB and VB values of semiconductors decide the intensity and type of ROS produced by light excitation [77]. Besides, in terms of thermodynamic viewpoint, both semiconductors have the ability to be photoexcited and then reduce CO2 to CO and CH4. However, as shown in Fig. S11, SnS2 was incapable of generating CH4 even after long irradiation (λ＞400), suggesting that effective accumulation and extraction of photoinduced electrons in SnS2 are not allowed, most likely due to fast charge recombination. For the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 hybrid, if electrons transferred through the conventional heterojunction route, the photoinduced electrons of g-C3N4 would transfer to the CB of SnS2. Consequently, CH4 will not be produced. Meanwhile, when irradiated by the visible light, e--h+ pairs are photogenerated in SnS2 and g-C3N4. The photoexcited h+ probably remain in SnS2 VB, whereas the e- in SnS2 CB transfers to VB of g-C3N4 due to ohmic contact. The e- in g-C3N4 VB is further stimulated to its CB, which is beneficial to improve the efficiency towards the separation of e--h+ pairs. The h+ stored in SnS2 VB is trapped in H2O molecule to produce H+ and O2. Simultaneously, the e- in g-C3N4 CB is transmitted to its surface, which can increase the e- density of g-C3N4. The Z-type heterojunction constructed by SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 provides more photogenerated carriers and active substances, which has a similar trend to the signal in the ESR spectrum in Fig. 10 [78,79]. Notably, as revealed by the Zeta potential diagram (Table S2), the electric field inside the interface formed by the electrostatic interaction of the two semiconductors inevitably affects the electron transport, thereby changing the photocatalytic activity of the material [38]. In addition, We further studied the difference in charge density of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterostructure. The calculated charge density difference is shown in Fig. 11b. At the interface of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded heterostructure, the surface of g-C3N4 is mainly dominated by green areas, slightly covered by purple areas, and the surface of SnS2 is mainly filled by purple areas, partly occupied by the green area.

On the basic of the above consequence, the reasonable mechanism of the system to tremendously heighten the catalyst performance of visible light driving can be ascribed to the following sides: (1) The use of a specific energy band structure coupled with g-C3N4 expands the light absorption range and utilization. In addition, the Z-type mechanism constructed to provide more photogenerated electrons and holes pairs to participate in the process of photocatalytic reduction of CO2; (2) Here, Au nanoparticles enter the SnS2/g-C3N4 embedded structure, which utilizes the good conductivity of Au nanoparticles to provide a channel for carrier transport. When excited by light, Au nanoparticles produce more excited electrons, and photogenerated electrons are more easily transferred in CB of SnS2 due to sufficient energy to cause electron transitions in the HOMO level. The internal electric field of the interface formed by the SnS2/g-C3N4 embedded lay structure plays an important role in it.

In summary, the SnS2 doped with carbon and nitrogen hybridization during the experimental synthesis proved the successful coupling of g-C3N4. Observed obvious face-to-face SnS2/g-C3N4 embedded layer morphology, and the uniformly distributed Au nanoparticles improved the conductivity of hybrid photocatalysts. The representative detection of SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 during light excitation has been studied by PL spectroscopy. The energy band structure based on g-C3N4 material though efficient modulation, the synthesized SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 exhibited significant photoexcitation in photo-living oxygen; •OH and •O2 − are the main active substances and the formation Z-type mechanism generates more electrons when excited by light, thereby improving the reaction efficiency. The FE-TEM clearly shows the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure. This work delivers a novel tactics for the construction of the internal electric field thanks to electrostatic interaction of the interface of 2D semiconductors. Meanwhile, photocurrent, impedance, LSV curve, steady-state fluorescence and transient fluorescence results show that Au nanoparticles not only act as a bridge for electron transport in 2D semiconductor materials to accelerate electron transport, but generate more excited electrons by themselves, which effectively boosts the separation of carriers and enhances the catalyst activity. In addition, DFT calculations show that the Z-type mechanism and the intercalated energy level of the SnS2/Au/g-C3N4 embedded structure expand the light absorption and improve the carrier separation efficiency.
